Homage: Special honor or respect shown publicly. Obeisance: Deferential respect or a gesture expressing deference. Supplication: The action of asking or begging for something humbly or earnestly. Acquiescent: Ready to accept or agree to something without protest. Implore: To beg earnestly or piteously for help or mercy. Meek: Quiet, gentle, submissive, and easily imposed upon. Reverence: Deep respect, awe, or devotion for someone or something.

Group Meaning & Context: To Take, Misappropriate, or Steal Property
Words with Meanings: Appropriate: To take possession of something for one's own use, often without permission. Misappropriate: To dishonestly or illegally take money or property for personal use. Expropriate: To take property away from its owner for public or private use. Egocentric: Thinking only of oneself, without regard for the feelings or desires of others. Usurp: To take a position of power or importance illegally or by force. booty: Valuable stolen goods, loot, or pillage taken in war or robbery. Extort: To obtain something by force, threats, or unfair pressure. Larceny: The theft of personal property. Plunder: To steal goods from a place or person, typically using force. Embezzlement: The theft or misappropriation of funds placed in one's trust. Exploit: To make full use of a resource, or a bold daring feat.

Commensurate: Corresponding in size, degree, or proportion; equal. Disinterested: Not biased or influenced by personal interest; impartial. Egalitarian: Believing in the principle that all people are equal and deserve equal rights. Equitable: Fair and impartial; dealing fairly and equally with all concerned. Homogeneous: Of the same kind; alike in structure or composition. Impartial: Treating all rivals or disputants equally; fair and unbiased. Reciprocal: Given, felt, or done in return; mutual. Reciprocate: To respond to a gesture or action by making a corresponding one. Tantamount: Equivalent in seriousness to; virtually the same as. Uniform: Remaining the same in all cases and at all times; unchanging. Parallel: Side by side and having the same distance continuously between them. Peers: People of the same age, status, or ability.

Inclement: Unpleasantly cold, wet, harsh, or severe weather. Relentless: Unceasingly intense, harsh, or unforgiving. arid: Having little or no rain; extremely dry, parched, or lacking interest. climatic: Relating to climate, weather conditions, or environment. Raucous: Making or constituting a disturbingly harsh and loud noise.

Culinary: Relating to cooking, food preparation, or the kitchen. cuisine: A style or method of cooking characteristic of a particular country. delectable: Delicious, highly pleasing, or delightful to the senses. Epicure: A person who takes refined pleasure in fine food and drink. Fodder: Raw material, food for livestock, or material used for a purpose. Gastronomy: The practice or art of choosing, cooking, and eating good food. Palatable: Pleasant or acceptable to the taste or mind. Pungent: Having a sharply strong taste or smell. Voracious: Wanting or devouring great quantities of food or possessing an insatiable appetite. Goblet: A drinking glass with a foot and stem. Ingest: To swallow, take in, or absorb food or liquid into the body. Succulent: Tender, juicy, and tasty food or plant.

Group Meaning & Context: Highest Point, Peak, or Ultimate Climax
Words with Meanings: Apotheosis: The perfect example, highest development, or elevation to divine status. Culminate: To reach the highest point, final climax, or decisive result. Eminent: Famous and respected within a particular sphere or profession. Exalt: To hold someone or something in very high regard; to praise or elevate. Heyday: The period of a person's or thing's greatest success, popularity, or vigor. Pivotal: Of crucial importance in relation to the development or success of something. Preeminent: Surpassing all others; distinguished or supreme in a field. Salient: Most noticeable or important; prominent. Sublime: Of such excellence, grandeur, or beauty as to inspire awe. Transcend: To go beyond or rise above a range or limit. apex: The highest point, peak, or climax of something. apogee: The highest point in the development of something; climax. climactic: Forming an exciting, decisive, or crucial climax. crux: The decisive, central, or most important point at issue. Extremity: The furthest point or limit, or a state of extreme hardship. Zenith: The time at which something is most powerful or successful; peak. Ascendant: Rising in power, influence, or status. Triumph: A great victory, achievement, or state of joyful success. Unrivalled: Having no equal or match; supreme and peerless.

Culpable: Deserving blame, guilty, or responsible for a wrong. Inculpate: To accuse, charge, or implicate in a crime or wrongdoing. Peccadillo: A small, relatively unimportant offense or sin. Recrimination: An accusation in response to one from someone else. Reprehensible: Deserving censure, condemnation, or severe scolding. Venial: Denoting a sin or mistake that is not regarded as depriving the soul of divine grace; pardonable. arraign: To call or bring a defendant before a court to answer a criminal charge. compunction: A feeling of guilt, moral regret, or scruple that prevents wrongdoing. conviction: A firmly held belief or opinion; or a formal declaration of guilt. delinquent: Failing in duty, negligent, or characterized by minor crime. Impeach: To formally charge a public official with misconduct in office. Indict: To formally accuse of or charge with a serious crime. Perpetrator: A person who carries out a harmful, illegal, or immoral act. Recidivism: The tendency of a convicted criminal to reoffend.

Group Meaning & Context: Freeing from Blame, Guilt, or Offense
Words with Meanings: Exculpate: To clear from blame, guilt, or accusation of wrongdoing. Absolve: To free from guilt, blame, or responsibility. Exonerate: To officially absolve someone from blame, fault, or wrongdoing. Extricate: To free or untangle someone or something from a constraint or difficulty. Reparation: The making of amends for a wrong one has done, by paying money or helping. Venial: Denoting a sin or mistake that is not regarded as depriving the soul of divine grace; pardonable. vindicate: To clear someone of blame, suspicion, or doubt. acquit: To free someone from a criminal charge by a verdict of not guilty. debunk: To expose the falseness or pretentiousness of an idea or belief. Emancipate: To set free from legal, social, or political restrictions. Exempt: Free from an obligation, duty, or liability required of others. Impunity: Exemption from punishment or freedom from injurious consequences. Nullify: To make legally null and void, invalidate, or cancel. Redeem: To compensate for the faults or bad aspects of something; save from evil. Redress: To remedy or set right an unfair or undesirable situation. Remission: A temporary diminution of the severity of disease or pain; forgiveness.

Cursory: Hasty and notice-lacking; performed without thoroughness or detail. Dilettante: A person who engages in an activity or interest without serious commitment or knowledge. Facile: Appearing neat and comprehensive only by ignoring the true complexities; superficial or easily achieved. Perfunctory: Carried out with a minimum of effort or reflection; superficial. Precipitous: Dangerously high or steep, or done suddenly and without careful consideration. Superficial: Existing or occurring at or on the surface; lacking depth. cursory: Hasty and therefore not thorough or detailed. Frantically: In a hurried, desperate, or wildly excited manner. Hasty: Done or acting with excessive speed, rush, or urgency. Imprudent: Lacking good judgment, wisdom, or caution; rash. Petty: Of little importance or trivial.

Daunt: To intimidate, discourage, or make someone feel apprehensive. Dismay: Consternation, distress, or anxiety caused by something unexpected. Dispirit: To cause to lose enthusiasm, hope, or courage. Dissuade: To persuade someone not to take a particular course of action. Deter: To discourage or prevent someone from taking action. Intimidate: To frighten, threaten, or overawe someone into submission. Startle: To cause to feel sudden shock, surprise, or alarm.

Group Meaning & Context: Scarcity, Lack, or Shortage of Something
Words with Meanings: Dearth: A scarcity, lack, or inadequate supply of something. Destitute: Extremely poor and lacking the means to provide for oneself. Indigent: Poor; needy; destitute. Paltry: Small or meager in amount; petty or trivial. Paucity: The presence of something only in small or insufficient quantities; scarcity. deficit: The amount by which something is too small or inadequate. deplete: To use up or exhaust the supply or resources of something. Impecunious: Having little or no money; impoverished. Impoverish: To make a person or area poor or exhausted of quality. Privation: A state in which essential things for human well-being are lacking. Scant: Barely sufficient or adequate in amount or quantity. Bereft: Deprived of or lacking something, especially a non-material asset. Deprivation: The damaging lack or denial of material benefits or necessities. Devoid: Entirely lacking or free from something. Dwindle: To diminish gradually in size, amount, or strength. Meager: Lacking in quantity, quality, or fullness; thin or sparse. Puny: Small and weak, or insignificant. Scarce: Insufficient for the demand; rare or hard to find. Scarcity: The state of being scarce or in short supply. Sparse: Thinly dispersed or scattered; scant.

Debacle: A sudden, disastrous collapse, humiliation, or complete failure. Abortive: Failing to accomplish an intended result; unsuccessful. Founder: To sink, fail completely, or collapse. Futile: Incapable of producing any useful result; pointlessly ineffective. calamity: An event causing great and often sudden damage, distress, or disaster. cataclysm: A large-scale and violent event in the natural world or political sphere. Fiasco: A complete, ignominious, or disastrous failure. Rout: A disorderly retreat of defeated troops, or a decisive defeat. Ruination: The state of being destroyed, ruined, or bankrupt. Upheaval: A violent, sudden, or dramatic disruption to a situation or order. Wreck: To ruin, destroy, or severely damage something.

Group Meaning & Context: Excessive Indulgence, Moral Decay, or Corruption
Words with Meanings: Debauchery: Excessive indulgence in sensual pleasures, vice, or dissipation. Decadent: Characterized by moral or cultural decline, luxury, and decay. Degenerate: Having lost physical, mental, or moral qualities; deteriorated. Depravity: Moral corruption or wickedness. Dissipate: To scatter, squander, or fade away gradually. Dissolution: The closing, dismissal, or breakdown of an official body, partnership, or moral bond. Hedonism: The pursuit of pleasure and self-indulgence as the highest good. Indulgent: Having or showing a readiness to be lenient or to gratify desires. Inordinate: Unusually or disproportionately large; excessive. Prodigal: Spending money or resources freely and recklessly; wastefully extravagant. Profligate: Recklessly extravagant or wasteful in the use of resources. Sordid: Involving ignoble actions and motives; dirty, squalid, or morally low. Squander: To waste something in a reckless and foolish manner. Surfeit: An excessive amount of something, especially food or drink. Turpitude: Depravity, wickedness, or corrupt behavior. Venal: Showing or motivated by susceptibility to bribery; corrupt. wanton: Deliberate, unprovoked, malicious, or sexually unrestrained. bacchanal: A wild, drunken, or riotous party or celebration. benighted: In a state of pitiful or contemptible intellectual or moral ignorance. cloy: To disgust or sicken someone with an excess of sweetness or richness. Lascivious: Feeling or revealing overt and often offensive sexual desire. Licentious: Promiscuous and unprincipled in sexual matters. Prurient: Having or encouraging an excessive interest in sexual matters. Revel: To enjoy oneself in a lively and noisy way, especially with drinking and dancing. Ribald: Referring to sexual matters in an amusingly rude or irreverent way. Voluptuous: Characterized by luxury or sensual pleasure, or curvaceous in shape. Extravagant: Lacking restraint in spending money or using resources. Graft: Corruption or bribery in politics, or joining plant tissues together. Hedonist: A person who believes that the pursuit of pleasure is the highest good. Improvident: Lacking foresight; careless or wasteful with resources. Indulge: To allow oneself or another to enjoy a pleasure or desire freely. Prodigality: Wasteful or reckless extravagance in spending. Sleazy: Sordid, corrupt, immoral, or cheap and poorly made. Splurge: To spend money extravagantly or ostentatiously. Unsavoury: Disagreeable, unpleasant, or morally objectionable.

Group Meaning & Context: To Weaken, Enfeeble, or Wear Down
Words with Meanings: Debilitate: To make someone or something weak, feeble, or infirm. Attrition: A gradual wearing down, weakening, or reduction in strength. Enervate: To cause someone to feel drained of energy or vitality; weaken. Impotent: Lacking power, strength, or vigor; helpless. Languish: To grow weak or feeble; to suffer neglect or hardship. Lethargy: A lack of energy, enthusiasm, or alertness. Tenuous: Very weak or slight, insubstantial, or flimsy. Attenuate: To reduce the force, effect, or value of; weaken. atrophy: To waste away, decline, or deteriorate from disuse or disease. Dotage: A period of feebleness of mind or senility in old age. Emaciate: To make abnormally thin or weak by lack of food. Susceptible: Likely or liable to be influenced or harmed by a particular thing. Undermine: To weaken or damage subversively or gradually. Wane: To decrease vigor, power, size, or extent; become weaker. Faint: Barely perceptible; lacking clarity, strength, or consciousness. Falter: To lose strength or momentum; speak or move hesitantly. Fragile: Easily broken, damaged, or delicate in structure. Handicap: A circumstance or condition that makes progress or success difficult. Ineffectuality: The quality of being ineffective, useless, or lacking power. Rust: A reddish-brown coating formed on iron by oxidation, or to deteriorate through neglect. Senile: Showing weakness or memory loss characteristic of old age. Wither: To dry up, decay, shrivel, or fade away from loss of vitality.

Decorous: Polite, proper, and keeping with good taste and propriety. Genteel: Polite, refined, or respectable, often in an affected or ostentatious way. Propriety: The state or quality of conforming to conventionally accepted standards of behavior or morals. Urbane: Suave, courteous, and refined in manner. composed: Calm, tranquil, and in control of one's feelings or emotions. demeanor: Outward behavior, bearing, or conduct toward others. demure: Reserved, modest, shy, and quiet in manner. Mores: The essential customs and conventions of a community. Protocol: The official system of rules and acceptable behavior. Punctilious: Showing great attention to detail or correct behavior. Salutation: A gesture or utterance made as a greeting or acknowledgment. Delicate: Easily broken or damaged, fragile, or requiring tact.

Deduce: To arrive at a fact or conclusion by reasoning or logic. Extrapolate: To estimate or conclude something by extrapolating existing trends or data. Infer: To deduce or conclude information from evidence rather than from explicit statements. Postulate: To suggest or assume the existence, fact, or truth of something as a basis for reasoning. Premise: A previous statement or proposition from which another is inferred or follows. ascertain: To find out for certain, discover, or make sure of. deem: To regard, consider, or judge in a specified way. delve: To reach inside, search, or research deeply into a subject. Fallacy: A mistaken belief or a flaw in logical reasoning. Ratiocination: The process of exact thinking and logical reasoning. Rationale: A set of reasons or a logical basis for a course of action or a particular belief.

Group Meaning & Context: To Slander, Defame, or Damage Reputation
Words with Meanings: Defame: To damage the good reputation of someone by slander or libel. Malign: To speak ill of, defame, or slander someone maliciously. Invective: Insulting, abusive, or highly critical language. Libel: A published false statement that is damaging to a person's reputation; written defamation. Slander: The action or crime of making a false spoken statement damaging to a person's reputation. Slander: The action or crime of making a false spoken statement damaging to a person's reputation. Stigmatize: To describe or regard as worthy of disgrace or great disapproval. vilify: To speak or write about in an abusively disparaging or slanderous manner. aspersion: A disparaging or derogatory remark intended to harm someone's reputation. calumny: The making of false and defamatory statements to damage a reputation. decry: To publicly denounce, condemn, or express strong disapproval of. denounce: To publicly declare to be wrong, evil, or deserving condemnation. Revile: To criticize in an abusive or angrily insulting manner. Denunciation: Public condemnation of someone or something.

Definitive: Conclusive, authoritative, final, and providing a decisive answer. Dictum: A formal or authoritative statement or pronouncement. Injunction: An authoritative warning or order, especially a judicial court order. Irrevocable: Not able to be changed, reversed, or recovered; final. Decisive: Settling an issue, producing a definite result, or firm in decision.

Delineate: To describe, portray, or outline precisely. denote: To be a sign, symbol, or indication of; signify. depict: To show, represent, or portray by a drawing or written description. Render: To provide, give, or cause to become. Portrait: A drawing, painting, or photograph of a person.
Delude: To deceive, fool, or trick someone into believing what is false. Beguile: To charm, enchant, or trick someone, sometimes deceptively.

Group Meaning & Context: A Great Flood or Overwhelming Rush
Words with Meanings: Deluge: A severe flood, overwhelming downpour, or massive rush of things. Glut: An excessively abundant supply of something. Inundate: To overwhelm someone with things or people; to flood. barrage: A concentrated artillery bombardment or overwhelming outpouring of questions. brouhaha: A noisy, overexcited, or exaggerated reaction or response. Engulf: To sweep over something so as to surround or overwhelm it completely.

Group Meaning & Context: Relating to Mind, Thought, or Brain
Words with Meanings: Cerebral: Intellectual, analytical, or pertaining to the brain rather than emotion. Cognitive: Relating to mental processes of perception, memory, and reasoning. Cognizant: Aware, mindful, or having knowledge of something. Dialectical: Relating to logical discussion of ideas and opinions through dialogue or opposing forces. Existential: Relating to human existence, meaning, or survival. Introvert: A shy, reticent person who prefers solitary activities. Introspective: Examining one's own conscious thoughts and feelings. Lucid: Expressed clearly; easy to understand, or sane/rational. Pensive: Engaged in, involving, or reflecting deep or serious thought. Profound: Very great or intense; having or showing great knowledge or insight. Ruminate: To think deeply about something; ponder or turn over in the mind. Sagacious: Having or showing keen mental discernment and good judgment; wise. Sage: A profoundly wise person, especially one featured in ancient history or legend. Sentient: Able to perceive or feel things; conscious. cogitate: To think deeply about something; ponder or meditate. Fathom: To understand or comprehend a difficult concept after thought. Muse: To be absorbed in thought or consider deeply; a source of inspiration. Premeditated: Thought out or planned beforehand. Psyche: The human soul, mind, or spirit. Ratiocination: The process of exact thinking and logical reasoning. Retrospect: A survey or review of a past course of events or period of time. Underlying: Situated beneath or serving as the fundamental basis of something. Apprehend: To arrest someone for a crime, or to understand/perceive. Cognizance: Knowledge, awareness, or notice of something. Deliberation: Long and careful consideration or discussion. Grasp: To seize and hold firmly, or to comprehend fully. Intuitive: Instinctive, based on inner feeling rather than explicit reasoning. Perceive: To become aware or conscious of something through the senses or mind. Rumination: A deep or considered thought about something.

Catholic: All-inclusive, broad-minded, or universal in extent. Comprehensive: Including or dealing with all or nearly all elements or aspects. Eclectic: Deriving ideas, style, or taste from a broad and diverse range of sources. Exhaustive: Including or considering all elements or aspects; fully comprehensive. Permeate: To spread throughout every part of something; pervade. Pervade: To spread through and be perceived in every part of. Ubiquitous: Present, appearing, or found everywhere. Gamut: The complete range or scope of something. Generic: Characteristic of or relating to a whole class or group; general. Spectrum: A band of colors or a wide range of related ideas or properties. Sweeping: Wide-ranging or extended in scope; taking in a wide area. Systemic: Relating to or affecting a system as a whole. Pervasive: Spreading widely throughout an area or a group of people. Prevalent: Widespread or commonly occurring in a particular area or time.

Parochial: Narrow-minded, limited in scope, or provincial. Insular: Ignorant of or uninterested in cultures, ideas, or peoples outside one's own experience; isolated. Myopia: Nearsightedness or a narrow-minded lack of foresight. Parochial: Having a limited or narrow outlook or scope; provincial. Philistine: A person who is hostile or indifferent to culture and the arts. Provincial: Lacking sophistication; narrow-minded or belonging to a province. Tangential: Diverging from a previous line or course; barely relevant. Purblind: Having defective vision; slow or deficient in understanding.

Banal: Lacking originality, commonplace, boring, and trite. Cliché: A phrase, idea, or opinion that is overused and lacks originality. Hackneyed: Lacking significance through having been overused; unoriginal and trite. Platitude: A remark or statement, especially one with a moral content, that has been used too often to be interesting. bromide: A trite, overused, or unoriginal remark made to soothe or placate. Hoary: Ancient, greyish-white, or overused. Litany: A tedious recital or repetitive series of complaints, prayers, or chants. Trite: Overused, lacking originality, or commonplace.

Group Meaning & Context: Difficult, Strenuous, or Requiring Hard Work
Words with Meanings: Arduous: Requiring strenuous effort, difficult, and exhausting. Exacting: Making great demands on one's skill, care, or attention; strict. Onerous: Involving an amount of effort and difficulty that is oppressively burdensome. Ponderous: Slow and clumsy because of great weight; dull or laborious. Toil: To work extremely hard or incessantly. Ordeal: A severe, painful, difficult, or grueling trial or experience. Strenuous: Requiring or using great exertion or energy. Torturous: Involving, causing, or characterized by severe pain or suffering.

Ardent: Passionate, enthusiastic, and full of intense feeling. Ebullient: Cheerful and full of energy; exuberantly enthusiastic. Effusion: An unrestrained expression of feelings or an outpouring. Fervor: Intense and passionate feeling or enthusiasm. Incandescent: Emitting light as a result of being heated; passionate or brilliant. Inflame: To provoke or intensify strong feelings, passion, or anger. Vehement: Showing strong feeling; forceful, passionate, or intense. vociferous: Vehement, loud, and clamorous in expressing one's opinions. zealous: Having or showing great enthusiasm, fervor, or devotion in pursuit of a cause. Paroxysm: A sudden attack or violent outburst of a particular emotion or activity. Rapture: A feeling of intense pleasure or joy. Ardor: Great enthusiasm, passion, or fervor. Fervent: Having or displaying a passionate intensity. Kindle: To ignite a fire, or to arouse and inspire emotion or passion. Zeal: Great passion, fervor, and enthusiasm in pursuit of a cause or objective. Zealot: A person who is fanatical and uncompromising in pursuit of their ideals. Zestful: Full of vibrant energy, enthusiasm, and lively enjoyment.

Aesthetic: Pertaining to beauty, art, or the appreciation of good taste. Sensuous: Affecting the senses in a pleasing way; aesthetic. Sublime: Of such excellence, grandeur, or beauty as to inspire awe. baroque: Highly ornate, extravagantly detailed, or overly elaborate in style. delectable: Delicious, highly pleasing, or delightful to the senses. Exquisite: Extremely beautiful, delicate, or finely made. Lyrical: Expressing deep emotions in an imaginative and beautiful manner. Resplendent: Attractive and impressive through being richly colorful or sumptuous. Sumptuous: Splendid and expensive-looking; luxurious. Voluptuous: Characterized by luxury or sensual pleasure, or curvaceous in shape. Ornate: Elaborately or excessively decorated. Portrait: A drawing, painting, or photograph of a person. Radiant: Shining, glowing, or emitting bright light or joy. Splendor: Magnificent, splendid, or grand appearance. Svelteness: The quality of being slender, elegant, and graceful.

Affinity: A natural liking for, attraction to, or similarity with someone or something. Analogy: A comparison between two different things to highlight similarities. Penchant: A strong or habitual liking for something or a tendency to do something. Predilection: A preference or special liking for something; a bias. Prepossess: To influence or bias beforehand, or to preoccupy the mind favorably. Propensity: An inclination or natural tendency to behave in a particular way. Empathy: The ability to understand and share the feelings of another. Inclination: A person's natural tendency or urge to act or feel in a certain way. Predispose: To make someone liable or inclined to a specified attitude or condition. Enamour: To cause to be filled with love or admiration for.

Group Meaning & Context: Short Amusing Story or Illustrative Account

Vignette: A brief evocative description, account, or episode. Fable: A short story, typically with animals, conveying a moral. Jest: A humorous remark, joke, or playful banter.

Group Meaning & Context: Concise Wise Saying or Universal Truth

Dictum: A formal or authoritative statement or pronouncement. Epigram: A pithy, witty saying or remark expressing an idea in a clever way. Epigraph: An inscription on a building, statue, or coin, or a short quotation at the start of a book. Epithet: An adjective or descriptive phrase expressing a quality characteristic of the person or thing mentioned. Homily: A religious discourse or sermon intended for spiritual or moral edification. Precept: A general rule intended to regulate behavior or thought. Tenet: A principle or belief, especially one of the main principles of a religion or philosophy. adage: A traditional proverb or short statement expressing a general truth. Sententious: Given to moralizing in a pompous or affected manner. Verity: A true principle or belief, or the state of being true.

Group Meaning & Context: To Cause, Pester, Harass, or Surround
Words with Meanings: Beleaguer: To harass, trouble, surround, or beset with difficulties. Beset: To trouble, harass, surround, or threaten persistently. Exasperate: To irritate intensely; frustrate or provoke to anger. Foment: To instigate or stir up an undesirable or violent sentiment or course of action. Instigate: To bring about or initiate an action or event, especially a bad one. vex: To annoy, frustrate, or worry someone. Entreat: To ask someone earnestly or anxiously to do something. Harass: To subject to aggressive pressure, intimidation, or torment. Harry: To persistently harass, raid, or annoy. Importune: To harass someone persistently or pressingly for something. Provocation: Action or speech that makes someone annoyed or angry. Aggravate: To make a problem, injury, or offense worse or more serious. Engulf: To sweep over something so as to surround or overwhelm it completely. Environ: To surround or enclose. Vexatious: Causing or tending to cause annoyance, frustration, or distress.

Blatant: Done openly and shamelessly; flagrant and obnoxiously obvious. Egregious: Outstandingly bad, shocking, or flagrant. Enormity: The extreme scale, seriousness, or outrageous nature of something evil. Flagrant: Conspicuously offensive, blatant, or scandalous. Flaunt: To display something ostentatiously, especially in order to provoke envy or admiration. Flout: To openly disregard or mock a rule, law, or convention. Patent: Easily perceived or obvious; clear and open. arrant: Complete, utter, and thoroughgoing in an infamous sense. brazen: Bold, shameless, audacious, and insolent. conspicuous: Standing out so as to be clearly visible or attracting attention. Obtrusive: Noticeable or prominent in an unwelcome or intrusive way. Glaring: Blatantly obvious, highly conspicuous, or staring fiercely.

Callow: Inexperienced, immature, and lacking wisdom or sophistication. Dilettante: A person who engages in an activity or interest without serious commitment or knowledge. Rudimentary: Involving or limited to basic principles; undeveloped. benighted: In a state of pitiful or contemptible intellectual or moral ignorance. Embryonic: In an early, undeveloped, or rudimentary stage. Fledgling: A person or organization that is immature, young, or inexperienced. Layman: A person without professional or specialized knowledge in a subject. Neophyte: A person who is new to a subject, skill, or belief; beginner. Precocious: Having developed certain abilities or proclivities at an earlier age than usual. Sophomoric: Juvenile, immature, and pretentious. Rookie: A person who is new or inexperienced in an activity or profession. Sophomore: A student in the second year of high school or college, or an inexperienced person.

Cajole: To coax, flatter, or persuade someone gently into doing something. blandishment: Flattering or pleasing statements used to persuade someone gently. Fawn: To display exaggerated affection or flattery to gain favor. Induce: To succeed in persuading or leading someone to do something. Ingratiate: To bring oneself into favor with someone by flattering them. Flatter: To praise excessively or insincerely to gain favor. Implore: To beg earnestly or piteously for help or mercy. Tempt: To entice or invite someone to do something unwise or wrong.

Compelling: Evoking interest, attention, or admiration in a powerful way. Compulsion: An irresistible urge or force driving someone to act. Group Meaning & Context: Willing to Please, Agreeable, or Responsive
Words with Meanings: Amenable: Open, agreeable, and responsive to suggestion or advice. Docile: Ready to accept control or instruction; submissive. Obsequious: Obedient or attentive to an excessive, servile degree. Servile: Having or showing an excessive willingness to serve or please others. Sycophant: A person who acts obsequiously toward someone to gain advantage. Unctuous: Excessively flattering or ingratiating; oily. accede: To agree, yield, or assent to a demand, request, or treaty. comply: To act in accordance with a wish, command, rule, or request. concede: To admit that something is true, valid, or surrendered after denying it. Group Meaning & Context: To State, Declare, or Assert Openly
Words with Meanings: Allege: To assert or claim without proof or confirmation. Avow: To declare openly, assert, or acknowledge frankly. Profess: To claim open allegiance to or declare openly. Promulgate: To promote or make widely known an idea, cause, or law. Tout: To attempt to sell something, typically by pestering people in an aggressive manner. assert: To state a fact or belief confidently and forcefully. Pontificate: To express one's opinions in an pompous or dogmatic way. Proclaim: To announce officially or publicly. Propound: To put forward an idea, theory, or point of view for consideration. Thesis: A statement or theory that is put forward as a premise to be maintained or proved. Enunciate: To express an idea clearly; pronounce words distinctly. Insistence: The act of demanding or asserting something firmly and persistently. Posit: To put forward as a basis for argument or principle.

Ambience: The general atmosphere, mood, or character of a place. Zeitgeist: The defining spirit or mood of a particular period of history. Environ: To surround or enclose. Somber: Dark, gloomy, serious, or solemn in mood.

Group Meaning & Context: Relating to Love, Romance, or Passion
Words with Meanings: Amorous: Showing, feeling, or relating to romantic or sexual desire. Infatuated: Possessed with an intense but short-lived passion or admiration. conjugal: Relating to marriage or the formal relationship between married partners. Endear: To cause to be liked or loved. Mawkish: Sentimental in a feeble, exaggerated, or sickly way. Platonic: Intimate and affectionate but not romantic or sexual. Prurient: Having or encouraging an excessive interest in sexual matters. Titillate: To stimulate or excite someone, especially in a sexual or pleasurable way. Tryst: A private romantic rendezvous or secret meeting between lovers. Enamour: To cause to be filled with love or admiration for. Fling: To throw or hurl forcefully; a brief casual romance.

Amorphous: Lacking a definite shape, form, or clear structure. Ethereal: Extremely delicate and light in a way that seems too perfect for this world; heavenly. Ineffable: Too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words. Lump: An irregular mass of matter, or to combine into a single group. Vague: Unclear, indefinite, or lacking precise meaning or form.

Amoral: Lacking moral sense or unconcerned with right and wrong. Depravity: Moral corruption or wickedness. Illicit: Forbidden by law, rules, or custom; illegal. Infamous: Well known for some bad quality or deed; notorious. Nihilism: The rejection of all religious and moral principles, believing life is meaningless. Unconscionable: Not right or reasonable; unreasonably excessive or unscrupulous. wanton: Deliberate, unprovoked, malicious, or sexually unrestrained. callous: Showing or having an insensitive and cruel disregard for others. crass: Lacking sensitivity, refinement, or intelligence. delinquent: Failing in duty, negligent, or characterized by minor crime. Licentious: Promiscuous and unprincipled in sexual matters. Perverse: Showing a deliberate and obstinate desire to behave unacceptably. Reprobate: An unprincipled or wicked person. Renegade: A person who deserts and betray an organization, country, or set of principles. Sleazy: Sordid, corrupt, immoral, or cheap and poorly made.

Antithesis: The exact opposite or direct contrast of something. Ironic: Happening in a way contrary to what is expected, causing wry amusement. Juxtapose: To place or deal with two things close together for contrasting effect. antipodal: Directly opposite or situated on the opposite side of the earth. converse: To engage in conversation; or the opposite or contrary of a statement.

Apprehensive: Anxious, fearful, or uneasy about the future. Misapprehension: A mistaken belief, misunderstanding, or wrong idea. Diffident: Modest or shy because of a lack of self-confidence. Introvert: A shy, reticent person who prefers solitary activities. Perturb: To make someone anxious or unsettled; disturb greatly. Trepidation: A feeling of fear or agitation about something that may happen. aghast: Filled with horror, shock, or amazement. angst: A feeling of deep anxiety, dread, or persistent apprehension. appalling: Causing shock, dismay, horror, or disgust. blanch: To flinch, grow pale, or draw back from shock, fear, or heat. consternation: Feelings of anxiety, dismay, or distress at something unexpected. cower: To crouch down in fear, terror, or submissiveness. craven: Contemptibly lacking in courage; cowardly. cringe: To bend one's head and body in fear or servile humility. Disquiet: A feeling of anxiety, worry, or unease. Ghastly: Causing great horror, fear, or revulsion; frightful. Paranoia: Unreasonable suspicion and distrust of others. Presentiment: An intuitive feeling about the future, especially a foreboding. Skittish: Excitable or easily scared; nervous or unpredictable. Specter: A ghost or something widely feared as a possible unpleasant event. Timorous: Showing or suffering from nervousness, fear, or a lack of confidence. Agitate: To make someone troubled or nervous, or stir up violently. Bashful: Reluctant to draw attention to oneself; shy or reserved. Dither: To be indecisive or hesitant. Frantically: In a hurried, desperate, or wildly excited manner. Startle: To cause to feel sudden shock, surprise, or alarm.

Assiduous: Showing great care, attention, and persistent effort. Painstaking: Done with or employing great care, thoroughness, and effort. Peruse: To read or examine something carefully and thoroughly. Resolute: Admirably purposeful, determined, and unwavering. Scrupulous: Diligent, thorough, and extremely attentive to details; highly honest. Scrutinize: To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly. Tenacious: Tending to keep a firm hold of something; persistent or stubborn. Unremitting: Never relaxing or slackening; incessant. Habituate: To make accustomed or used to something through repetition. Meticulous: Showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise. Stalwart: Loyal, reliable, and hardworking. Toil: To work extremely hard or incessantly. Conscientious: Wishing to do one's work or duty well and thoroughly. Dogged: Having or showing tenacity and grim persistence. Persistent: Continuing firmly in a course of action despite difficulty.

Auspicious: Showing or suggesting that future success is likely; favorable. Fortuitous: Happening by a lucky or unexpected chance. Propitious: Giving or indicating a good chance of success; favorable. Sanguine: Optimistic or positive, especially in an apparently difficult situation. Serendipity: The occurrence and development of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way. Intrepid: Fearless; adventurous and bold. augur: To portend, predict, or foreshadow a good or bad outcome. Inauspicious: Unfavorable, unpromising, or indicating bad luck.

Group Meaning & Context: Resembling or Relating to an Uncle
Words with Meanings: Avuncular: Resembling or characteristic of a benevolent, kindly uncle.
Awry: Off correct course, crooked, or away from expected order. Roundabout: Indirect or circuitous in phrasing or path.

Bastion: A strong defense, stronghold, or fortified place. Impervious: Not allowing fluid to pass through; unable to be affected by. asylum: A place of safety, refuge, or protection from danger or persecution. bulwark: A defensive wall, rampart, or person serving as a protection. citadel: A fortress, typically on high ground, protecting a city. Impenetrable: Impossible to pass through, enter, or understand. Impregnable: Unable to be captured, broken into, or defeated. Insuperable: Impossible to overcome or surmount. Palisade: A fence of wooden stakes forming a defense or barrier. Untenable: Incapable of being defended, maintained, or justified against criticism.

Group Meaning & Context: To Dwell on or Explain Excessively
Words with Meanings: Belabor: To explain or insist on excessively, or to attack verbally. Elucidate: To make something clear; explain. Harp: To talk or write persistently and tediously on a topic. Rumination: A deep or considered thought about something.

Bemused: Puzzled, confused, or mildly amused in thought. Enigma: A person or thing that is mysterious, puzzling, or difficult to understand. Labyrinth: A complicated irregular network of passages or paths; a maze. Obfuscate: To deliberately render unclear, confusing, or difficult to understand. bedlam: A scene of noisy uproar, confusion, chaos, or madness. confound: To cause surprise, confusion, or perplexity in someone. conundrum: A confusing, difficult, or intricate problem or riddle. Discomfit: To make someone feel embarrassed, confused, or uneasy. Disconcert: To disturb the composure of; unsettle or confuse. Quandary: A state of perplexity or uncertainty over what to do in a difficult situation. Stupor: A state of near-unconsciousness or insensibility. Transfix: To cause someone to become motionless with horror, wonder, or astonishment. Turbid: Cloudy, opaque, or thick with suspended matter; confused or obscure. Bemuse: To puzzle, confuse, or bewilder someone. Bewilder: To cause someone to become perplexed and confused. Reel: To lose balance, sway, or feel dazed and confused. Stupefy: To make someone unable to think or feel clearly; astonish or daze.

Bequest: A gift of property or money left to someone in a will. Bequeath: To leave or pass on property or wisdom to others in a will. Legacy: An amount of money or property left to someone in a will; long-lasting impact. Patrimony: Property inherited from one's father or male ancestor. Posterity: All future generations of people. Progeny: A descendant or the descendants of a person, animal, or plant.

Blasphemy: Disrespectful or profane speech/action against sacred or religious things. Extraneous: Irrelevant or unrelated to the subject being dealt with. Heresy: Opinion profoundly at odds with accepted paramount beliefs or orthodox doctrine. Iconoclast: A person who attacks cherished beliefs or traditional institutions. Profane: Relating or devoted to that which is not sacred; disrespectful of religion. Sacrilege: Violation or misuse of what is regarded as sacred. Sanctimonious: Making a show of being morally superior to other people. malediction: A magical word or phrase uttered with the intention of bringing about evil; a curse. Heretical: Holding beliefs contrary to established religious or conventional doctrines.

Clique: A small, exclusive group of people who do not readily allow outsiders. Esoteric: Intended for or likely to be understood by only a small number of people with specialized knowledge. Faction: A small, organized, dissenting group within a larger one. Genre: A category of artistic, musical, or literary composition characterized by a particular style. Insurgent: A rebel or revolutionary rising in active revolt. cabal: A secret political clique, faction, or conspiracy. caucus: A conference or meeting of members of a political party or group. cohort: A group of people banded together or treated as a group. confederate: A person or state joined with another in a league, alliance, or conspiracy. constituency: A body of voters or supporters represented by an elected official. coterie: A small, exclusive group of people with shared interests or tastes. Elite: A select group superior in ability, wealth, or status. Junta: A military or political group that rules a country after taking power by force. Sect: A subgroup of a religious, political, or philosophical belief system. Shibboleth: A custom, principle, or belief distinguishing a particular class or group of people. Triumvirate: A group of three powerful individuals holding power jointly.

Coerce: To compel, force, or intimidate someone to do something. Duress: Threats, violence, or constraints used to compel someone. Extort: To obtain something by force, threats, or unfair pressure. Repress: To subdue or restrain someone or something by force. Shackle: A metal ring or chain used to fasten a prisoner's wrists or ankles. Suppress: To forcibly put an end to or prevent the development or expression of. Throttle: To attack or choke someone by strangling; or to control/suppress energy. Intimidate: To frighten, threaten, or overawe someone into submission.

Colloquial: Used in ordinary or familiar informal conversation. Dialectical: Relating to logical discussion of ideas and opinions through dialogue or opposing forces. Gesticulate: To use gestures, especially dramatic ones, instead of speaking or to emphasize one's words. Homily: A religious discourse or sermon intended for spiritual or moral edification. vernacular: The native language or common dialect of a specific region or group. bandy: To pass on or discuss rumors or ideas in a casual or careless manner. banter: Playful, lighthearted, and good-humored teasing or conversation. cant: Hypocritical and sanctimonious talk, or specialized jargon of a group. confer: To grant or bestow a title, or hold a discussion to exchange opinions. converse: To engage in conversation; or the opposite or contrary of a statement. Discourse: Written or spoken communication or debate. Prattle: To talk in a foolish or simple-minded way for a long time. Repartee: Conversation or speech characterized by quick, witty comments or replies. Retort: To answer to a remark or accusation, typically in a sharp, angry, or witty manner. Grapevine: An informal, unofficial, or rumor-based network of communication. Mutter: To speak softly and unclearly, typically in complaint or discontent.

Complacent: Smug, overly self-satisfied, and unconcerned with potential dangers. Conceit: Excessive pride in oneself or a fanciful expression. Gloat: To dwell on one's own success or another's misfortune with smug pleasure.

Group Meaning & Context: To Complete or Perfect Something Else
Words with Meanings: Complement: Something that completes, perfects, or brings to perfection. Integral: Necessary to make a whole complete; essential or fundamental.

Group Meaning & Context: To Make Up, Form, or Constitute
Words with Meanings: Comprise: To consist of, include, or contain as parts. Compose: To write, create, or constitute by combining parts. Constitute: To form, establish, or make up a whole. Group Meaning & Context: Inborn, Present from Birth, or Innate
Words with Meanings: Congenital: Present from birth, innate, or ingrained habit. Inherent: Existing in something as a permanent, essential, or characteristic attribute. Innate: Inborn; natural; existing from birth. Intrinsic: Belonging naturally; essential; inherent. Intuitive: Instinctive, based on inner feeling rather than explicit reasoning.

Conjecture: An opinion or conclusion formed on the basis of incomplete information. Extrapolate: To estimate or conclude something by extrapolating existing trends or data. Hypothetical: Supposed but not necessarily real or true; based on a hypothesis. Postulate: To suggest or assume the existence, fact, or truth of something as a basis for reasoning. Premise: A previous statement or proposition from which another is inferred or follows. Putative: Generally considered, supposed, or reputed to be. Tentative: Not certain or fixed; provisional or hesitant. Presumably: Used to convey what is very likely or probable. Presuppose: To tacitly assume or require as a precondition beforehand. Sacrosanct: Regarded as too important or sacred to be interfered with. Divine: Of, from, or like a god; sacred or heavenly. Consonant: In agreement, harmony, or accord with something. Fraternal: Relating to or like a brother or brothers; friendly and supportive. Homogeneous: Of the same kind; alike in structure or composition. Pertinent: Relevant or applicable to a particular matter; apposite. accede: To agree, yield, or assent to a demand, request, or treaty. accord: An official agreement, treaty, or harmonious concurrence. apropos: Very appropriate, relevant, or opportune to a situation. apposite: Apt, highly appropriate, and well-suited to the circumstances. apt: Suitable, appropriate, or having a natural tendency to do something. compatible: Able to exist, occur, or work together without conflict. concert: Joint action, harmony, or agreement formed by union. Germane: Relevant, pertinent, or appropriate to a subject. Seamless: Smooth and continuous, with no apparent gaps or awkward transitions. Concur: To be of the same opinion or agree. Congruent: In agreement or harmony; identical in form and size. Consent: Permission for something to happen or agreement to do something. Inured: Accustomed or hardened to hardship, pain, or difficulty over time. Pertain: To be appropriate, related, or applicable to. Reconcile: To restore friendly relations or make consistent.

Construe: To interpret or analyze a statement or action in a particular way. decipher: To succeed in understanding, interpreting, or converting coded text. Explicate: To analyze and explain a text or idea in detail.

Consummate: Showing high degree of skill; complete or perfect in every detail. Eminent: Famous and respected within a particular sphere or profession. Impeccable: In accordance with the highest standards of faultless propriety; flawless. Preeminent: Surpassing all others; distinguished or supreme in a field. Prodigy: A person, especially a young one, endowed with exceptional qualities or abilities. Proficient: Competent or skilled in doing or using something. Robust: Strong and healthy; vigorous or sturdy. Transcend: To go beyond or rise above a range or limit. virtuoso: A person highly skilled in music, art, or another technical field. artisan: A skilled manual worker or craftsperson. competent: Having necessary ability, knowledge, or skill to do something successfully. Effectual: Producing or capable of producing the desired result. Exquisite: Extremely beautiful, delicate, or finely made. Inimitable: So good or unusual as to be impossible to copy or imitate. Paragon: A person or thing regarded as a perfect example of a particular quality. Quintessential: Representing the most perfect or typical example of a quality or class. Adept: Very skilled or proficient at something. Dazzle: To amaze or overwhelm with impressive quality, skill, or bright light. Immaculate: Perfectly clean, neat, pure, or free from flaws. Contiguous: Sharing a common border, touching, or immediately adjacent. Juxtapose: To place or deal with two things close together for contrasting effect. Proximity: Nearness in space, time, or relationship.

Group Meaning & Context: Remorseful, Penitent, or Sorry for Sin

Penitent: Feeling or showing sorrow and regret for having done wrong. atone: To make amends or reparation for a wrong, crime, or sin. compunction: A feeling of guilt, moral regret, or scruple that prevents wrongdoing. Conventional: Based on or in accordance with general agreement or standards. Mundane: Lacking interest or excitement; dull, ordinary, or commonplace. Nominal: Existing in name only, or very small and token in amount. Orthodox: Conforming to what is traditionally accepted as right or true. Uniform: Remaining the same in all cases and at all times; unchanging. Utilitarian: Designed to be useful or practical rather than attractive. Vapid: Offering nothing that is stimulating or challenging; dull. bland: Lacking strong features or characteristics, insipid, or uninteresting. classic: Judged over a period of time to be of the highest quality and outstanding. Quotidian: Of or occurring every day; ordinary or mundane. Vogue: The prevailing fashion or style at a particular time. Humdrum: Lacking excitement or variety; dull and monotonous. Lackluster: Lacking brilliance, energy, vitality, or enthusiasm. Monotonous: Dull, tedious, repetitive, and lacking in variety.

Group Meaning & Context: Logical Result, Consequence, or Inevitable Outcome

Repercussion: An unintended consequence occurring after an event or action. Covenant: A formal, binding agreement, contract, or promise. Fidelity: Faithfulness to a person, cause, or belief, demonstrated by continuing loyalty. Injunction: An authoritative warning or order, especially a judicial court order. Stipulate: To demand or specify a requirement, typically as part of an agreement. allegiance: Loyalty or commitment of a subordinate to a superior or cause. breach: An act of breaking or failing to observe a law, agreement, or wall. Truce: A temporary agreement to suspend fighting or cease hostilities.

Group Meaning & Context: Standard, Principle, or Benchmark for Judging

Epitome: A person or thing that is a perfect example of a particular quality or type. Exemplify: To be a typical example of or illustrate by giving an example. Genre: A category of artistic, musical, or literary composition characterized by a particular style. Hierarchy: A system in which members of an organization or society are ranked according to status. Ideology: A system of ideas and ideals, especially one forming the basis of economic or political theory. Paradigm: A typical example, model, or pattern of something. Precedent: An earlier event or action that is regarded as an example or guide in similar circumstances. Precept: A general rule intended to regulate behavior or thought. Qualitative: Relating to, measuring, or measured by the quality of something. Tenet: A principle or belief, especially one of the main principles of a religion or philosophy. Touchstone: A standard or criterion by which something is judged or recognized. canon: A general law, rule, principle, or criterion by which something is judged. classic: Judged over a period of time to be of the highest quality and outstanding. critique: A detailed analysis and assessment of something. decree: An official order issued by a legal authority. deem: To regard, consider, or judge in a specified way. Protocol: The official system of rules and acceptable behavior. Regimen: A prescribed course of medical treatment, way of life, or diet. Penchant: A strong or habitual liking for something or a tendency to do something. Predilection: A preference or special liking for something; a bias. Prodigy: A person, especially a young one, endowed with exceptional qualities or abilities. Propensity: An inclination or natural tendency to behave in a particular way. Efficacy: The ability to produce a desired or intended result. Forte: A thing at which someone excels; a strong point. Group Meaning & Context: To Appreciate or Increase in Value

Increment: An increase or addition, especially one of a series on a fixed scale. appraise: To assess the value, quality, or performance of something. assess: To evaluate, estimate, or judge the nature, quality, or value of.

Ambiguous: Open to more than one interpretation; unclear or vague. Elliptical: Obscure or ambiguous in expression; shaped like an ellipse. Equivocal: Open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous or uncertain. Equivocate: To use ambiguous language so as to conceal the truth or avoid committing oneself. Nebulous: Vague, hazy, indistinct, or ill-defined. Oblique: Indirect, slanting, or not explicit in direction or meaning. Obscure: Not discovered or known about, or uncertain and hard to understand. Opaque: Not able to be seen through; impossible to understand or explain. Double Entendre: A word or phrase open to two interpretations, one often suggestive. Nebulous: Vague, ill-defined, or in the form of a cloud. Vague: Unclear, indefinite, or lacking precise meaning or form.

Ambivalent: Having mixed, contradictory, or conflicting feelings about something. Equivocal: Open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous or uncertain. Vacillate: To alternate or waver between different opinions or actions; be indecisive. Dither: To be indecisive or hesitant. Falter: To lose strength or momentum; speak or move hesitantly. Bourgeois: Relating to the middle class, often with conventional or materialistic values. Materialistic: Excessively concerned with material possessions rather than spiritual values.

Words with Meanings: Broach: To raise or introduce a sensitive subject for discussion.

Figment: A thing that someone believes to be real but exists only in imagination. Phantasm: A figment of the imagination; an illusion or apparition. Incense: To make extremely angry or cause enraged feelings. Inflammatory: Arousing or intended to arouse violent, angry, or hostile feelings.
