Group Meaning & Context: To Direct Speech or Attention to a Matter
Words with Meanings: Address: To direct one's attention or speech to a specific matter or audience. Propound: To put forward an idea, theory, or point of view for consideration.

Adherent: A devoted supporter, follower, or advocate of a cause or leader. Espouse: To adopt or support a cause, belief, or way of life. Partisan: A strong, often prejudiced supporter of a party, cause, or person. Proponent: A person who advocates a theory, proposal, or project. Proselytize: To convert or attempt to convert someone from one religion, cause, or belief to another. Staunch: Loyal, committed, and firm in attitude or principle. Steadfast: Resolutely or dutifully firm and unwavering. advocate: To publicly recommend, support, or urge a cause or policy. constituency: A body of voters or supporters represented by an elected official. Interloper: A person who becomes involved in a place or situation where not wanted. Protege: A person who is guided and supported by an older or more experienced person. Patron: A person who gives financial or other support to a person, cause, or activity. Zealot: A person who is fanatical and uncompromising in pursuit of their ideals.

Group Meaning & Context: To Corrupt or Pollute by Adding Inferior Materials
Words with Meanings: Adulterate: To make impure or weaker by adding inferior substances. vitiate: To spoil, impair, or reduce the quality or effectiveness of. defile: To spoil, violate, or desecrate the purity or sanctity of something. Taint: To contaminate or pollute with a trace of something bad. Tamper: To interfere with or alter something illegally or improperly.

Averse: Having a strong feeling of opposition, antipathy, or disinclination. aversion: A strong feeling of dislike, opposition, or antipathy towards something. Loath: Reluctant, unwilling, or disinclined. Rebuff: To reject someone or something in an abrupt or ungracious manner. Spurn: To reject with disdain or contempt. Reluctant: Unwilling and hesitant to take an action.

Group Meaning & Context: Combination of Metals or Diverse Elements
Words with Meanings: Alloy: A mixture of two or more metals, or a combination of elements. Eclectic: Deriving ideas, style, or taste from a broad and diverse range of sources. Heterogeneous: Diverse in content, character, or origin. Amalgamate: To combine or unite to form one organization or structure.

Allusion: An indirect or passing reference to a person, place, or event. Implicit: Implied though not plainly expressed; understood without direct statement. Figurative: Departing from a literal use of words; metaphorical or symbolic. Imply: To strongly suggest the truth or existence of something not expressly stated. Insinuate: To suggest or hint something bad in an indirect and unpleasant way. Double Entendre: A word or phrase open to two interpretations, one often suggestive. Implication: A conclusion that can be drawn; an indirect suggestion. Innuendo: An allusive or oblique remark, typically suggestive or disparaging. Intimate: To state, hint, or imply indirectly; private or close. Inkling: A slight knowledge, vague suspicion, or subtle hint.
Amenity: A desirable feature, facility, or comfort that adds convenience. Expedient: Convenient and practical, although possibly improper or immoral.

Group Meaning & Context: To Restrict, Limit, or Draw Boundaries Around
Words with Meanings: Circumscribe: To restrict, limit, or draw a boundary around something. Fetter: To chain or shackle a prisoner; to restrict or restrain. Hindrance: A thing that provides resistance, delay, or obstruction to something or someone. Periphery: The outer edge, boundary, or margin of an area. Proscribe: To forbid, ban, or condemn officially. Qualify: To make competent or eligible, or to limit and modify a statement. Stipulate: To demand or specify a requirement, typically as part of an agreement. Stricture: A restriction or a sternly critical remark. Stringent: Strict, precise, and exacting in requirements or rules. curb: To restrain, check, or keep in control. Shackle: A metal ring or chain used to fasten a prisoner's wrists or ankles. Stint: A fixed, limited, or allotted period of time spent doing something. Constrain: To severely restrict the scope, extent, or activity of. Deter: To discourage or prevent someone from taking action. Hem: The folded and sewn edge of a cloth, or to enclose and restrict. Impediment: A hindrance, obstruction, or flaw in doing something. Rein: To check, restrain, or control an action or movement.

Group Meaning & Context: To Distribute, Assign, or Apportion Resources
Words with Meanings: Allocate: To distribute or assign resources for a specific purpose. allot: To give or apportion a specific share of something to someone. apportion: To divide and allocate shares of something among individuals. bestow: To confer, present, or grant an honor, right, or gift. deploy: To bring resources or troops into effective action or position. Stipend: A fixed regular sum paid as a salary or allowance. Consignment: A batch of goods delivered to someone to sell or manage.

Catharsis: The process of releasing and relieving strong, repressed emotions. Grandiose: Impressive or magnificent in appearance or style, especially pretentiously so. Cathartic: Providing psychological relief through open expression of strong emotions.

Denizen: An inhabitant or occupant of a particular place. Endemic: Native or restricted to a certain place or region. Indigenous: Originating or occurring naturally in a particular place; native. aboriginal: Inhabiting or existing in a land from the earliest times or from the beginning. demography: The study of statistics such as births, deaths, income, or disease. Aborigine: An original inhabitant of a region from earliest times. Demographic: Relating to the structure of populations. Inmate: A person confined to an institution such as a prison or hospital.

Didactic: Intended to teach, particularly in having moral instruction as an ulterior motive. allegory: A story, poem, or picture with a hidden symbolic or moral meaning. Explicate: To analyze and explain a text or idea in detail. Exposition: A comprehensive description and explanation of an idea or theory. Inculcate: To instill an attitude, idea, or habit by persistent instruction. Parable: A simple story used to illustrate a moral or spiritual lesson. Sententious: Given to moralizing in a pompous or affected manner.

Distend: To swell or cause to expand from within. Extrovert: An outgoing, socially confident person. Proliferate: To increase rapidly in numbers or multiply. Dilate: To make or become wider, larger, or more open. Bloat: To make swollen with fluid or gas, or inflate unpleasantly.

Dormant: In a state of temporary inactivity, sleep, or deep rest. Inert: Lacking the ability or strength to move or act; inactive. Respite: A short period of rest or relief from something difficult or unpleasant. Sloth: Indolence, laziness, or unwillingness to make effort. Soporific: Tending to induce drowsiness or sleep. Stagnation: The state of not flowing, moving, or changing; lack of activity or growth. Static: Lacking in movement, action, or change. Torpor: A state of physical or mental inactivity; lethargy. abeyance: A state of temporary disuse, suspension, or inactivity. Derelict: Abandoned, neglected, or failing in duty. Doldrums: A state or period of inactivity, stagnation, or depression. Ennui: A feeling of listlessness, boredom, and dissatisfaction. Listless: Lacking energy, enthusiasm, or direction. Quiescent: In a state or period of inactivity or dormancy. Repose: A state of rest, sleep, or tranquility. Sedentary: Tending to spend much time seated; somewhat inactive. Stolid: Calm, dependable, and showing little emotion or animation. Stupor: A state of near-unconsciousness or insensibility. Supine: Lying face upward; failing to act or protest as a result of moral weakness. Tedium: The state of being tedious, monotonous, or boring. Tepid: Showing little enthusiasm or warmth; lukewarm. Indolence: Habitual laziness, inactivity, or avoidance of effort. Lackluster: Lacking brilliance, energy, vitality, or enthusiasm. Languid: Displaying or having a disinclination for physical exertion; slow and relaxed.

Emigrate: To leave one's own country to settle permanently in another. Immigration: The action of coming to live permanently in a foreign country. Expatriate: A person who lives outside their native country. Repatriate: To return someone to their own country of origin. Exodus: A mass departure or exit of people. Influx: An arrival or entry of large numbers of people or things.

Enfranchise: To give the right to vote or confer a privilege/freedom. Disfranchise: To deprive someone of the right to vote or rights/privileges. Perquisite: A privilege or benefit enjoyed as a result of one's official position. Prerogative: A right or privilege exclusive to a particular individual or class. Proprietary: Relating to an owner, ownership, or exclusive rights. Emancipate: To set free from legal, social, or political restrictions. Empower: To give authority, power, or confidence to someone. Inalienable: Not able to be taken away or transferred to another owner. Suffrage: The right to vote in political elections. Deregulate: To remove government controls or regulations from.

Epitaph: A phrase or statement written in memory of a person who has died, especially on a tombstone. commemorate: To recall and show respect for someone or something in a ceremony. Monumental: Massive, highly significant, impressive, or enduringly important.

Exigency: An urgent need, demand, or emergency situation. brink: The extreme edge or verge of a steep place or critical point. Dilemma: A difficult situation or problem requiring a choice between bad options. Dire: Extremely serious, urgent, or terrible. Impasse: A situation in which no progress is possible; deadlock. Imperative: Of vital importance; crucial or giving an authoritative command. Paroxysm: A sudden attack or violent outburst of a particular emotion or activity. Plight: A dangerous, difficult, or otherwise unfortunate situation. Precarious: Not securely held or in position; dangerously likely to fall or collapse. Predicament: A difficult, unpleasant, or embarrassing situation. Thorny: Full of difficulties, complex issues, or controversial points. Turmoil: A state of great disturbance, confusion, or uncertainty.

Fatuous: Silly, foolish, and pointlessly foolish or stupid. Inane: Silly, stupid, or lacking sense or meaning. buffoon: A ridiculous but amusing person, fool, or clown. Dolt: A stupid or foolish person. Droll: Amusing in an odd or whimsical way. Flippant: Not showing a serious or respectful attitude. Folly: Lack of good sense; foolishness or stupidity. Jocular: Fond of or characterized by joking; humorous or playful. Ludicrous: So foolish, unreasonable, or out of place as to be amusing. Sophomoric: Juvenile, immature, and pretentious. Vacuous: Having or showing a lack of thought or intelligence; mindless. Fuss: A state of unnecessary excitement, activity, or minor concern. Goof: A foolish or careless mistake, or to make a silly blunder.

Flora: The plants of a particular region, habitat, or geological period. conservatory: A room or school dedicated to instruction in music, art, or plants. Verdant: Green with grass or other rich vegetation. Grape-vine: A climbing plant producing grapes, or an informal channel of communication. Succulent: Tender, juicy, and tasty food or plant. Twig: A small, slender branch or shoot from a tree or shrub. Wither: To dry up, decay, shrivel, or fade away from loss of vitality.

Itinerant: Traveling from place to place, especially for work. Itinerary: A planned route or journey schedule. Peripatetic: Traveling from place to place, especially working in various places. circumnavigate: To sail or travel completely around something. Exodus: A mass departure or exit of people. Jaunt: A short excursion or journey for pleasure. Meander: To follow a winding course or wander aimlessly. Nomadic: Living the life of a nomad; wandering from place to place. Odyssey: A long, wandering, and eventful journey. Peregrination: A long journey or travel from one place to another. Pilgrimage: A pilgrim's journey to a sacred place. Fugitive: A person who has escaped from captivity or is in hiding to avoid arrest. Stroll: Walk in a leisurely, relaxed way.

Kinetic: Relating to or resulting from motion or movement. Precipitate: To cause an event or situation to happen suddenly, unexpectedly, or prematurely. Robust: Strong and healthy; vigorous or sturdy. brawn: Physical strength, muscle, and power in contrast to intelligence. Diffuse: To spread or scatter widely; wordy or dispersed. Fuel: To supply with material to sustain or intensify. Galvanize: To shock or excite someone into taking sudden action. Imbue: To inspire, saturate, or permeate with a feeling or quality. Infuse: To fill, pervade, or instill a quality into something. Momentum: The quantity of motion or driving force gained by a moving body. Oscillate: To move or swing back and forth in a regular rhythm. Sally: A sudden charge out from a besieged place against an enemy. Waft: To pass or cause to pass easily or gently through the air. Agility: Ability to move quickly and easily or think rapidly. Alight: To descend from a train, bus, or other form of transport, or settle on a surface. Brisk: Quick, energetic, and active. Celerity: Swiftness of movement; speed. Conveyor: A person or thing that transports or communicates something. Dart: To move or run somewhere suddenly and rapidly. Fling: To throw or hurl forcefully; a brief casual romance. Hurtle: To move or cause to move at rapid, uncontrolled speed. Quiver: To tremble or shake with a slight rapid motion. Reel: To lose balance, sway, or feel dazed and confused. Retreat: To withdraw or move back from a position or conflict. Rub: To apply pressure and friction to a surface back and forth. Rustle: Make a soft, light, dry sound while moving. Scuttle: To run hurriedly with short quick steps, or to deliberately sink/abandon a plan. Stroll: Walk in a leisurely, relaxed way. Stumble: To trip or momentarily lose balance while walking. Tremor: An involuntary shaking movement or a slight seismic vibration.

Group Meaning & Context: Legal Proceedings, Lawsuits, and Court Action
Words with Meanings: Litigate: To resort to legal action to settle a matter. Perjury: The offense of willfully telling an untruth in a court after taking an oath. Rebut: To claim or prove that evidence or an accusation is false. affidavit: A written statement confirmed by oath or affirmation for use as legal evidence. arraign: To call or bring a defendant before a court to answer a criminal charge. Forensic: Relating to the application of scientific methods to legal problems. Impeach: To formally charge a public official with misconduct in office. Indict: To formally accuse of or charge with a serious crime. Punitive: Inflicting or intended as punishment. Sanction: Official permission or approval for an action, or a penalty for disobeying a law. Warrant: To justify or necessitate a course of action, or an official authorization. Annul: To declare invalid an official agreement, decision, or result. Apprehend: To arrest someone for a crime, or to understand/perceive. Enact: To make a bill or proposal into law; perform. Extradition: The surrender of an alleged or convicted criminal to another jurisdiction. Fugitive: A person who has escaped from captivity or is in hiding to avoid arrest. Misdemeanor: A minor offense or criminal infraction less serious than a felony. Prosecute: To conduct legal proceedings against someone.

Mortify: To humiliate, embarrass, or cause severe shame. abase: To lower in rank, office, prestige, or esteem; degrade. affront: An action or remark that causes outrage, insult, or offense. contretemps: An unexpected and unfortunate or embarrassing event or mishap. degrade: To treat or regard someone with contempt or lower their status. Discomfit: To make someone feel embarrassed, confused, or uneasy. Gaffe: An unintentional act or remark causing embarrassment; blunder.

Neologism: A newly coined word, phrase, or expression. acronym: An abbreviation formed from the initial letters of other words. appellation: A name, title, or designation given to a person or thing. Idiom: A phrase whose meaning is not predictable from its individual words. Jargon: Special words or expressions used by a profession that are hard to understand. Malapropism: The mistaken use of a word in place of a similar-sounding one. Nomenclature: A system of names or terms used in a particular field. Oxymoron: A figure of speech in which contradictory terms appear together. Pseudonym: A fictitious name, especially one used by an author. Syntax: The arrangement of words and phrases to create well-formed sentences. Connotation: An idea or feeling that a word invokes in addition to its literal meaning. Linguist: An expert in foreign languages or the formal study of linguistics.

Nepotism: Favoritism shown to relatives or friends by those in power. Invidious: Likely to arouse resentment, envy, or anger in others. Cronyism: The practice of favoring personal friends, especially by giving them jobs.

Novel: New, original, or unusual in an interesting way. advent: The arrival or creation of a notable person, thing, or event. avant-garde: New and unusual or experimental ideas, styles, or art. Inimitable: So good or unusual as to be impossible to copy or imitate. Revamp: To give new and improved structure, form, or appearance to. Singular: Exceptionally good or remarkable; unique or unusual. Vogue: The prevailing fashion or style at a particular time.

Paternal: Showing kindness and care associated with a father; fatherly. Patrimony: Property inherited from one's father or male ancestor. Posterity: All future generations of people. Progeny: A descendant or the descendants of a person, animal, or plant. Offspring: A child or descendant of a parent, animal, or plant.

Pathology: The science of the causes and effects of diseases or abnormal conditions. affliction: A cause of persistent pain, distress, suffering, or hardship. apoplexy: Extreme anger or rage leading to incapacity; or stroke. atrophy: To waste away, decline, or deteriorate from disuse or disease. Fester: To become worse or rot; produce pus. Pandemic: An outbreak of a disease prevalent over a whole country or the world. Placebo: A harmless substance given as a dummy treatment. Regimen: A prescribed course of medical treatment, way of life, or diet. Trauma: A deeply distressing or disturbing experience or physical injury. Antidote: A medicine taken or given to counteract a particular poison. Haggard: Looking exhausted, gaunt, or unwell from fatigue or suffering. Hospice: A facility providing care and comfort for terminally ill patients. Immune: Protected or exempt from a particular disease, obligation, or penalty. Senile: Showing weakness or memory loss characteristic of old age.

Prerequisite: A thing that is required as a prior condition for something else to happen. Requisite: Made necessary by particular circumstances or regulations. Entail: To involve, require, or necessitate as a consequence. Imperative: Of vital importance; crucial or giving an authoritative command. Incumbent: Necessary as a duty or responsibility; currently holding office. Presuppose: To tacitly assume or require as a precondition beforehand. Suffice: To be enough or adequate for a purpose.

Protagonist: The leading character or one of the major characters in a drama or story. allegory: A story, poem, or picture with a hidden symbolic or moral meaning. anthology: A published collection of poems, stories, or other pieces of writing. Epilogue: A section or speech at the end of a book or play. Prologue: An introductory section or speech of a literary work. Motif: A dominant or recurring idea, image, or feature in a work. Vignette: A brief evocative description, account, or episode. Fable: A short story, typically with animals, conveying a moral.
Secular: Denoting attitudes, activities, or other things that have no religious basis. Temporal: Relating to worldly as opposed to spiritual affairs; or relating to time.

Sensory: Relating to sensation or the physical senses. Sensual: Relating to or fulfilling physical and especially sexual appetites. Sensuous: Affecting the senses in a pleasing way; aesthetic. Sentient: Able to perceive or feel things; conscious. corporeal: Relating to a person's physical body as opposed to the spirit. corporal: Relating to the human body; physical. Olfactory: Relating to the sense of smell. Pungent: Having a sharply strong taste or smell. Purblind: Having defective vision; slow or deficient in understanding. Waft: To pass or cause to pass easily or gently through the air. Numb: Deprived of physical sensation, feeling, or responsiveness. Perceive: To become aware or conscious of something through the senses or mind. Vivid: Producing clear, detailed, bright, and powerful mental images.

Solvent: Having assets ample to meet debts; able to pay all debts. Insolvent: Unable to pay debts owed; bankrupt. afford: To have enough money, time, or means for something; or to grant or provide. annuity: A fixed sum of money paid to someone each year. arrears: Money that is owed and should have been paid earlier. capital: Chief, primary, or principal; or wealth and assets used for investment. deficit: The amount by which something is too small or inadequate. denomination: A recognized autonomous branch of a religion or a face value unit. Fiscal: Relating to government revenue, taxes, and financial matters. Remuneration: Money paid for work or a service. Stipend: A fixed regular sum paid as a salary or allowance. Subsidize: To support financially or pay part of the cost of. Underwrite: To sign and accept financial responsibility or guarantee support for. Usury: The illegal action or practice of lending money at unreasonably high rates of interest. Barter: To exchange goods or services for other goods or services without using money.

Surrogate: A substitute, especially a person appointed to act for another. Emissary: A person sent on a special diplomatic mission. Supersede: To take the place of a person or thing previously in authority or use. Titular: Holding or constituting a purely formal position or title without real authority.

Theology: The study of the nature of God and religious belief. apostasy: The formal abandonment or renunciation of a religious or political belief. benediction: An utterance of good wishes, blessing, or grace before a meal. canon: A general law, rule, principle, or criterion by which something is judged. deity: A god or goddess in a polytheistic religion or divine being. denomination: A recognized autonomous branch of a religion or a face value unit. Devout: Deeply religious, pious, or devoted. Divine: Of, from, or like a god; sacred or heavenly. Ecclesiastical: Relating to the Christian church or clergy. Fetish: An excessive or irrational devotion to a particular object. Karma: The sum of a person's actions deciding their fate in future existences. Litany: A tedious recital or repetitive series of complaints, prayers, or chants. Mystic: Relating to mystery or spiritual significance beyond human comprehension. Nirvana: A state of perfect peace, happiness, and liberation from suffering. Occult: Relating to mystical, supernatural, or magical powers/practices. Pilgrimage: A pilgrim's journey to a sacred place. Resurrection: The revitalization or revival of something, or coming back to life. Sect: A subgroup of a religious, political, or philosophical belief system. Supplication: The action of asking or begging for something humbly or earnestly. Anoint: To smear or rub with oil as part of a religious ceremony or to nominate officially. Congregation: A group of people assembled for religious worship or a gathering. Devotion: Love, loyalty, or enthusiasm for a person, activity, or cause. Halo: A circle of light shown around the head of a sacred person or object.

Utopia: An imagined place or state of things in which everything is perfect. Dystopia: An imagined state or society in which there is great suffering or injustice. visionary: Thinking about or planning the future with great wisdom or imagination. Nirvana: A state of perfect peace, happiness, and liberation from suffering.

vestige: A remaining trace, sign, or remnant of something that no longer exists. Dross: Something regarded as worthless or rubbish; waste. Patina: A green or brown film on the surface of bronze produced by oxidation over time. Wake: A trail of disturbed water left by a ship, or a consequence following an event. Debris: Scattered pieces of waste, remains, or rubbish. Fragment: A small part broken off or detached from something complete. Relic: An object surviving from an earlier time, especially one of historical interest.

viable: Capable of working successfully, surviving, or developing. Extant: Still in existence; surviving. Precarious: Not securely held or in position; dangerously likely to fall or collapse. Suffice: To be enough or adequate for a purpose.
vicarious: Experienced in the imagination through the actions or feelings of another. Subsidiary: Less important than but related or supplementary to something.

vocation: A strong feeling of suitability or calling for a particular career or trade. Entrepreneur: A person who organizes and operates a business taking financial risks. Impresario: A person who organizes or manages public entertainments. Consignment: A batch of goods delivered to someone to sell or manage. Linguist: An expert in foreign languages or the formal study of linguistics. Menial: Lowly, degraded, or requiring little skill or prestige. Peddler: A person who travels from place to place selling goods. Peddling: The activity of selling goods in small quantities while traveling.

volition: The faculty or power of using one's own will or making a choice. Self-Made: Having become successful or rich by one's own efforts. Unilateral: Performed by or affecting only one person, group, or country involved. Waive: To refrain from insisting on or applying a claim, right, or rule. Discretion: The quality of behaving or speaking in such a way as to avoid offense.

abase: To lower in rank, office, prestige, or esteem; degrade. debase: To reduce something in quality, character, or value. degrade: To treat or regard someone with contempt or lower their status. Opprobrious: Expressing scorn, disgrace, or severe criticism. Sordid: Involving ignoble actions and motives; dirty or squalid. Menial: Lowly, degraded, or requiring little skill or prestige.

abet: To encourage, assist, or support someone in committing a crime or wrongdoing. Protege: A person who is guided and supported by an older or more experienced person. Reclaim: To retrieve or recover something that was lost or taken. Solace: Comfort or consolation in a time of distress or sadness. Subsidize: To support financially or pay part of the cost of. Underwrite: To sign and accept financial responsibility or guarantee support for. Antidote: A medicine taken or given to counteract a particular poison. Handicap: A circumstance or condition that makes progress or success difficult. Intervention: Direct involvement in a situation to alter or prevent an outcome. Ministration: The provision of assistance, care, or service to others. Reinforce: To strengthen or support an existing position, idea, or object.

abomination: A thing that causes disgust, hatred, or intense loathing. anathema: Something or someone that is vehemently disliked, cursed, or shunned. canvass: To solicit votes, orders, or opinions from a group of people. Grimace: An ugly, twisted expression on a person's face expressing pain/disgust. Insufferable: Too extreme, unpleasant, or annoying to bear. Noisome: Having an extremely offensive or disgusting smell; harmful. Odious: Extremely unpleasant; repulsive or deserving hatred. Repugnant: Extremely distasteful or unacceptable; offensive. Revulsion: A sense of disgust and loathing. Scorn: The feeling or belief that someone or something is worthless or despicable. Despise: To feel contempt or deep repugnance for. Loathsome: Causing intense hatred, disgust, or revulsion. Repulsive: Arousing intense distaste, disgust, or aversion. Slimy: Covered in unpleasantly wet slippery substance, or repulsive and treacherous.

accentuate: To make more noticeable, emphasize, or prominent. Predominant: Present as the strongest or main element. Preponderance: The quality or fact of being greater in number, quantity, or importance. Rivet: To hold completely one's attention or engross firmly. Transfix: To cause someone to become motionless with horror, wonder, or astonishment. Underscore: To emphasize or show the importance of something. Emphasize: To give special importance or prominence to something. Exude: To discharge or radiate a quality, emotion, or moisture steadily. Glaring: Blatantly obvious, highly conspicuous, or staring fiercely.

access: The means or opportunity to approach, enter, or use something. Threshold: A strip of wood or stone forming the bottom of a doorway; a point of entry. Verge: An edge or border, or the point at which something is about to happen. Induct: To formally admit someone into an organization, position, or role. Invade: To enter forcefully in order to conquer, occupy, or overrun.

accouterments: Additional items of dress or equipment required for an activity. appurtenance: An accessory or auxiliary item associated with a particular activity or lifestyle. Fodder: Raw material, food for livestock, or material used for a purpose. Abacus: An oblong frame with rows of wires along which beads are slid, used for calculating. Goblet: A drinking glass with a foot and stem. Helm: A wheel or tiller for steering a ship, or a position of leadership. Rudder: A flat piece hinged vertically at the rear of a ship or aircraft for steering.

accrue: To accumulate or receive over time in regular or increasing amounts. aggrandize: To increase the power, status, wealth, or reputation of. Garner: To gather, collect, or accumulate over time. Osmosis: The gradual or unconscious process of assimilation or absorption. Procure: To obtain or get possession of something with care or effort. Propagate: To spread and promote an idea, theory, or knowledge widely. Rampant: Flourishing or spreading unchecked. Rife: Of common occurrence or widespread; full of. Suffuse: To gradually spread through or over something with fluid, color, or light. Teem: To be full of or swarming with something. Ascendant: Rising in power, influence, or status. Augmentation: The action or process of making or becoming greater in size or amount. Hoard: A stored supply of valuable items, or to accumulate secretly. Proliferation: Rapid increase in numbers or a large amount.

adjourn: To break off a meeting or proceeding with the intention of resuming later. Derelict: Abandoned, neglected, or failing in duty. Embargo: An official ban on trade or commercial activity. Impasse: A situation in which no progress is possible; deadlock. Impede: To delay, obstruct, or hinder the progress of. Interlude: An intervening period of time or a short pause in execution. Moratorium: A temporary prohibition or suspension of an activity. Temporize: To avoid making a decision or committing oneself in order to gain time. Hinder: To delay, obstruct, or impede the progress of someone or something.

adjunct: A thing added to something else as a supplementary or auxiliary part. ancillary: Providing necessary support to the primary activities of an organization or system. appendage: A secondary or subpart attached to a main structure or body. auxiliary: Providing supplementary or additional help and support. concomitant: Naturally accompanying or associated with something else. Subsidiary: Less important than but related or supplementary to something. Annotation: A note of explanation or comment added to a text or diagram.

ad-lib: To speak, perform, or compose spontaneously without preparation. Impromptu: Done without being planned, organized, or rehearsed. Improvise: To create, perform, or produce something spontaneously without preparation.

advent: The arrival or creation of a notable person, thing, or event. Emanate: To issue or spread out from a source. Embryonic: In an early, undeveloped, or rudimentary stage. Prologue: An introductory section or speech of a literary work. Genesis: The origin, coming into being, or mode of formation of something. Herald: A sign or messenger that something is about to happen. Impending: About to happen; imminent. Influx: An arrival or entry of large numbers of people or things. Overture: An introduction, proposal, or opening movement to something. Prelude: An action or event serving as an introduction to something more important. Resurrection: The revitalization or revival of something, or coming back to life. Apparent: Clearly visible or understood; seeming real or true. Commence: To begin or start something. Emergence: The process of coming into view or becoming known. Impend: To be about to happen or threaten imminently. Kindle: To ignite a fire, or to arouse and inspire emotion or passion.

advocate: To publicly recommend, support, or urge a cause or policy. auspices: Patronage, guidance, support, or sponsorship by an organization. Lobby: To seek to influence a politician or public official on an issue. Patron: A person who gives financial or other support to a person, cause, or activity. Reinforce: To strengthen or support an existing position, idea, or object.

affiliate: To officially attach or connect a person or organization to a larger body. correlation: A mutual relationship or connection between two or more things. alchemy: A medieval chemical philosophy aiming to transform base metals into gold or a magical process of transformation.

armament: Military weapons, equipment, and forces prepared for war. armistice: A formal agreement by warring parties to stop fighting; a truce. arsenal: A collection or storehouse of weapons, ammunition, or resources. bivouac: A temporary military encampment or shelter made without tents. brandish: To wave or flourish something, especially a weapon, as a threat or show. deploy: To bring resources or troops into effective action or position. arrears: Money that is owed and should have been paid earlier. Foreclose: To rule out or prevent in advance; repossess property. Impecunious: Having little or no money; impoverished. augur: To portend, predict, or foreshadow a good or bad outcome. Envision: To visualize or imagine as a future possibility. Forebode: To act as an advance warning or omen of something bad. august: Respected, impressive, majestic, and dignified in status. Elite: A select group superior in ability, wealth, or status. Estimable: Worthy of great respect and admiration. bauble: A small, showy, but cheap ornament or trinket. Ornate: Elaborately or excessively decorated. Petty: Of little importance or trivial. Trivial: Of little value, importance, or significance.

cache: A hiding place used especially for storing provisions or valuables. Concealment: The action of hiding something or preventing it from being known. Hoard: A stored supply of valuable items, or to accumulate secretly. Sequestered: Isolated, hidden away, or secluded from others.

canvass: To solicit votes, orders, or opinions from a group of people. Census: An official count or survey of a population. Demographic: Relating to the structure of populations. Reconnoiter: To make a military observation or preliminary survey of an area.

concave: Having an outline or surface that curves inward like the interior of a circle. concentric: Denoting circles, arcs, or shapes that share the same center. configuration: An arrangement of elements in a particular form, figure, or combination. corrugated: Shaped into alternate ridges and grooves for structural strength. continuum: A continuous sequence in which adjacent elements are not perceptibly different. Ensue: To happen or occur afterward or as a result. Extant: Still in existence; surviving. contraband: Goods that have been imported or exported illegally. Embargo: An official ban on trade or commercial activity. Tamper: To interfere with or alter something illegally or improperly.

demise: A person's death, or the end and failure of an enterprise. Dilapidated: In a state of disrepair or ruin as a result of age or neglect. Dirge: A mournful song or poem for the dead.
